main_ingredients: pastry shell (wheat flour, butter, sugar, egg)

serving.natural_language_portion: One taro orh nee tart piece weighing approximately 80 g, as typically served at Singaporean dessert shops and bakeries.

nutrient_source.title: Taro (Colocasia esculenta), cooked, and pastry tart shell components — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for taro (cooked), lard, sugar, and pastry shell, combined with HPB Singapore nutrient reference data for orh nee (yam paste dessert)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for taro orh nee tart as a composite product. Nutrient values are estimated by combining: (1) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for taro cooked (FDC ID 170071) providing taro paste macros; (2) USDA SR Legacy pastry shell data for the tart crust; (3) HPB Singapore Foodo reference for orh nee (yam paste) dessert as a cross-check. Proportions estimated as approximately 55% taro paste filling and 45% pastry shell by weight. Values represent a plausible central estimate for a standard 80 g tart piece.

gi_source.title: Taro (Colocasia esculenta) glycemic index — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for taro orh nee tart as a composite product. GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for taro (Colocasia esculenta, boiled), which reports GI approximately 53 (low). The pastry shell component (wheat flour-based) has a higher GI (~60–70), but the dominant filling is taro paste with fat and sugar moderating the glycemic response. A blended GI of 53 is used as a conservative analogous estimate reflecting the taro-dominant filling. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 29.2) / 100 ≈ 15.5, rounded to 15.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content and high saturated fat from lard and pastry butter
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit taro orh nee tart to occasional consumption, as each piece contributes approximately 76 mg sodium and significant saturated fat from lard-enriched filling and butter pastry, which may adversely affect blood pressure and vascular tone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (53) combined with significant carbohydrate and sugar load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume taro orh nee tart in small portions and infrequently, as one 80 g piece delivers approximately 29 g carbohydrate and 11 g sugar with a glycemic load of approximately 15.6, which can meaningfully raise postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lard and butter pastry, and moderate cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat taro orh nee tart as an occasional indulgence, given its saturated fat content of approximately 4.8 g per serving derived from lard in the orh nee filling and butter in the pastry shell, which may contribute to LDL cholesterol elevation with regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taro Orh Nee Tart is a Singaporean dessert pastry that combines the traditional Teochew yam paste dessert (orh nee) with a Western-style tart shell. The tart consists of a buttery, crumbly shortcrust pastry shell filled with orh nee — a smooth, velvety taro (Colocasia esculenta) paste that is slow-cooked with lard, shallot oil, and sugar until it achieves a rich, silky texture. The filling is characteristically sweet, earthy, and fragrant, with a melt-in-the-mouth consistency derived from the high fat content of the lard and the starchy nature of taro. The pastry shell provides a contrasting crisp, golden exterior. Orh nee is a classic Teochew banquet dessert traditionally served at weddings and festive occasions in Singapore and among the Teochew diaspora in Southeast Asia. The tart format is a modern adaptation that makes the dessert more portable and individually portioned, popular in Singaporean bakeries and dessert shops. Nutritionally, one 80 g tart piece provides approximately 228 kcal, 29 g carbohydrate, 11.6 g fat, and 2.6 g protein. The taro filling contributes dietary fibre, potassium, and vitamin B6. The high saturated fat from lard and butter, combined with moderate sugar content, makes this a treat to be consumed in moderation, particularly for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 15.6.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for taro orh nee tart as a composite product. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for taro (cooked) and pastry shell components, cross-referenced against HPB Singapore Foodo reference data for orh nee dessert. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled taro. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data from USDA SR Legacy is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level USDA SR Legacy data (taro cooked FDC ID 170071, pastry shell) and HPB Singapore Foodo orh nee reference, as no single approved-source composite entry exists for taro orh nee tart. Proportions assumed: approximately 55% taro paste filling, 45% pastry shell by weight. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as taro orh nee tart contains no animal-derived vitamin B12 sources in meaningful quantities; this is confirmed zero by component analysis. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy taro or pastry shell entries and is not expected to be a significant contributor. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this estimation. 5. trans_fat_g of 0.1 reflects a small residual from commercial shortening that may be used in pastry shells; this is a plausible trace estimate. 6. Halal status is marked false as traditional orh nee is prepared with lard (pork fat); halal-certified versions using vegetable shortening exist but cannot be assumed for this record without restaurant-specific confirmation. 7. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled taro; the composite tart GI may differ depending on pastry shell proportion and sugar content. Glycemic load per serving = (53 × 29.2 g carbohydrate per 80 g serving) / 100 = 15.5, reported as 15.6. 8. Serving size of 80 g is an estimate for a standard single-piece tart as sold at Singaporean bakeries; actual weight may vary by vendor.
